The Harmonizer

The finale ended with a band that plays plausible music forever and a confession: the machinery can generate, but it cannot listen. This encore turns the pipeline around. Sing — or hum, or whistle, or plug in a MIDI keyboard — and the browser hears the note, finds it on part four's scale, and answers with the harmony a backup singer would pick, in real time, riding every bend and wobble of your voice.

Two problems hide in that sentence. The first is hearing a pitch at all — which sounds like it should be the easy half and is not. The second is choosing what to sing back, and the whole series has already solved it.

Step 1 · The ear, rebuiltAutocorrelation, made honest

Part one ended on a warning that pitch is not a measurement but an inference — play harmonics at 220, 330, and 440 Hz with no 110 Hz in them anywhere, and you hear 110 Hz, because the ear reports the repetition rate that best explains the evidence. Any honest pitch detector must make the same inference. The naive approach — take a Fourier transform, read off the tallest bin — fails for exactly the missing-fundamental reason: the tallest partial of a sung note is frequently not the note.

So ask the question the ear asks: how well does this signal resemble itself, shifted in time? Slide the last 2,048 samples against themselves and score the overlap at every shift — the autocorrelation. A signal repeating every 4.5 milliseconds scores a peak at a shift of 4.5 ms, and at 9, and at 13.5: the period and its multiples, the harmonic ladder seen from the time domain. 

NSDF(τ) = 2·Σ x[i]·x[i+τ] / Σ (x[i]² + x[i+τ]²)   // 1.0 = perfect repeat, whatever the volume
period  = first peak within 10% of the tallest      // "first", or the octave below steals the prize

The two refinements are the McLeod pitch method, and both matter. The normalization (top line) makes the score a pure shape-similarity, immune to the singer getting louder or quieter mid-note — and it hands you the peak height as a free clarity score: 0.98 means "definitely a note", 0.6 means breath, consonant, or chair squeak, and the harmonizer simply declines to sing along with chair squeaks. The "first peak, not tallest peak" rule (second line) kills the classic failure where a shift of two periods scores slightly better than one and the detector answers an octave low. A final parabolic touch-up between samples gets the period to a fraction of a sample — a few cents of pitch precision, which the readout displays against the nearest tempered note.

Aside · From oil wells to "Believe"

The most famous autocorrelation pitch tracker came out of the oil industry. Andy Hildebrand spent years at Exxon processing seismic reflections — autocorrelation is how you find a buried echo's delay — then retooled the same mathematics to find and move the pitch of a voice. He called it Auto-Tune (1997). Used gently it is an invisible intonation nurse; cranked to zero glide time it became the robotic yodel of Cher's "Believe" one year later, and pop music has never fully put the toy down. This page's detector is the listening half of that machine, minus the correction.

Step 2 · The replyChoosing the harmony

Detection hands us a frequency, sixty times a second. The reply is pure review. Round to the nearest semitone (part three's grid). Snap to the nearest note of the chosen key — the harmonizer politely assumes you meant to be in the scale, the same assumption every backup singer makes about a lead. Find the snapped note's scale degree, and count two degrees up or down the seven-note ring (part four): a diatonic third — sometimes major, sometimes minor, whichever the key dictates at that degree, which is exactly why parallel thirds sound like harmony rather than transposition (part five). 

sung E4, key of C →  degree 3 of the ring → two rungs up → G4   // a minor third
sung C4, key of C →  degree 1            → two rungs up → E4   // a major one
harmony_freq = sung_freq × 2^(Δsemitones/12)                    // the third *rides the bend*

The last line is the difference between a harmonizer and a music box. The harmony's interval is computed on the grid, but it is applied to your actual frequency — vibrato, scoops, the quarter-tone you sag at the end of a phrase — so the backing voice tracks you like a singer with good ears, not like a keyboard with a transpose button.

Step 3 · The duetSing

Press 🎤 listen and allow the microphone (audio never leaves the tab — the analysis is the loop above, running locally). Hum a slow phrase: the white trace is you, the amber trace is the machine's reply, the gridlines are the key's scale with the roots labeled. Headphones help; failing that, modest volume — echo cancellation keeps the synth from harmonizing with itself, mostly. No microphone handy? The piano plays the same brain: click keys, or plug in a MIDI keyboard and it is wired up automatically. Cycle the key if your song disagrees with C, and try both thirds for the full choir effect.

Two honest limitations, both instructive. The detector is monophonic — sing a chord (or two people at once) and the NSDF sees a signal that doesn't repeat at any single period, clarity collapses, and the harmonizer abstains; polyphonic transcription is a genuinely hard open problem. And the snapping is only as good as the key you chose — sing chromatically and the machine's diatonic faith becomes audible. Both are the same lesson the whole series kept teaching: the algorithm doesn't know music, it knows the assumptions you handed it.

CodaThe loop closes

Six parts built a path from air pressure to songcraft, and the finale generated music out of that theory. The encore completes the circuit: sound in, theory applied, sound out, fast enough to feel like a partner. Every piece was already on the shelf — part one's repetition inference became the detector, part three's grid became the rounding, part four's ring became the reply, part five's voice-leading instincts chose the interval. The series ends as an instrument that needs one more player: you.

← Part six: The Infinite Jukebox · Back to part one: The Anatomy of a Note

Sound by Tone.js. Standing on the shoulders of: McLeod & Wyvill, "A Smarter Way to Find Pitch" (2005) · de Cheveigné & Kawahara, "YIN, a fundamental frequency estimator" (2002) · Hildebrand, US Patent 5,973,252 (1999)
